category|Category:Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} [[JA:美和神社 (瀬戸内市長船町東須恵)]] {{wikidata link|Q11608847}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Miwa Shrine | 画像=[[File:Osafune Miwa jinja 08.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|WD=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|WD=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|WD=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|WD=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|WD=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|WD=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|WD=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|WD=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|WD=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|WD=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|WD=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|WD=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|WD=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:瀬戸内市の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Setouchi, Okayama]][[Category:qq]]

Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|WD=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|WD=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|WD=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|WD=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|WD=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|WD=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|WD=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|WD=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|WD=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|WD=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|WD=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|WD=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|WD=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Setouchi, Okayama]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Setouchi, Okayama]][[Category:qq]]

category|Category:Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} [[JA:美和神社 (瀬戸内市長船町東須恵)]] {{wikidata link|Q11608847}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Miwa Shrine | 画像=[[File:Osafune Miwa jinja 08.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|WD=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|WD=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|WD=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|WD=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|WD=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|WD=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|WD=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|WD=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|WD=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|WD=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|WD=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|WD=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|WD=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Setouchi, Okayama]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Setouchi, Okayama]]

== * 2260005007938 == Commons category (P373) == * Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '岡山県瀬戸内市長船町東須恵1063', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} [[JA:美和神社 (瀬戸内市長船町東須恵)]] {{wikidata link|Q11608847}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Miwa Shrine | 画像=[[File:Osafune Miwa jinja 08.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|WD=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|WD=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|WD=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|WD=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|WD=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|WD=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|WD=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|WD=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|WD=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|WD=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|WD=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|WD=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|WD=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Setouchi, Okayama]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Setouchi, Okayama]]

Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|WD=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|WD=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|WD=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|WD=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|WD=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|WD=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|WD=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|WD=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|WD=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|WD=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|WD=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|WD=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|WD=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|WD=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|WD=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|WD=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Setouchi, Okayama]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Setouchi, Okayama]]

'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 16150516553003440296 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 2260005007938 == Commons category (P373) == * Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '岡山県瀬戸内市長船町東須恵1063', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} [[JA:美和神社 (瀬戸内市長船町東須恵)]] {{wikidata link|Q11608847}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Miwa Shrine | 画像=[[File:Osafune Miwa jinja 08.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|qid=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|qid=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}} Osafune-chō Higashisue 1063 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|31.13|N|134|8|41.61|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}、{{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭=Spring grand festival: May 9
Summer grand festival: July 15
Autumn grand festival: October 9 | 本殿=Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Okayama|label=Miwa Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Miwa Shrine''' (みわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osafune-chō Higashisue|Q7105505|qid=Q7105505|1=Osafune, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Setouchi|Q691691|qid=Q691691|1=Setouchi, Okayama}}, {{ill|lt=Okayama Prefecture|Q132936|qid=Q132936|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The enshrined deity is {{ill|qid=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|qid=Q10936701|qid=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=shikinaisha (small shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. There is another shrine of the same name in Osafune-chō, {{ill|lt=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Osafune-chō, Setouchi)|Q11608849|qid=Q11608849|lt=Miwa Shrine|1=Miwa Shrine (Fukusato, Setouchi)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank was gōsha. === Overview === It stands on Mt. Hirotaka (166{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|qid=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}). Since ancient times, Mt. Hirotaka was called Miwa-no-mine and, like {{ill|lt=Mount Miwa|Q860627|qid=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, was revered as a sacred mountain. At the mountain’s north foot are {{ill|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi)|Q11603502|qid=Q11603502|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (Setouchi city)}} and the Fudasaki Kofun group; at the south foot is the Sazarashi Kofun group. These {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|qid=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} show the surrounding area had been inhabited since ancient times. A legend explains the mountain’s name through Empress {{ill|lt=Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}’s beloved horse “Shirataka” (White Hawk). While the empress was staying by boat at Kaburazaki in {{ill|lt=Ushimado|Q204799|qid=Q204799|1=Ushimado, Okayama}}, the horse accidentally fell into the sea. It swam to the north shore, ran up the mountain, and died there. Local people buried it carefully on the mountain. The mountain then came to be called “Shirataka-yama,” which later changed in pronunciation to “Hirotaka-yama.” At the summit is an {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}, called Okutsu Iwakura. After shrine buildings were built, the sacred object was transferred to the honden. Around the iwakura, small stones are laid out in a square, each side 11 meters. According to shrine tradition, it was divided and invited from {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|Q705542|qid=Q705542|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakurai|Q127026|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The date is unknown. According to the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} and ''Bizen-koku Shikishakō'', its {{ill|lt=court rank for shrines|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was Junior Third Rank; records from {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|qid=Q6398|1=1394}}–{{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|qid=Q6305|1=1427}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|qid=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|qid=Q6679|1=1492}}–{{ill|lt=1501|Q6131|qid=Q6131|1=1501}}) list it as Senior Third Rank. Oral tradition, however, says it was relocated from Hirotaka Shrine in Shirikai, {{ill|lt=Oku-chō|Q7082701|qid=Q7082701|1=Oku, Okayama}}, in the same city''Oku-gun-shi, Part 2''. As a remnant of this, during the autumn grand festival, priests and attendants go to Shirikai, perform purification in seawater, and offer sake and rice to Hirotaka Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|qid=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, during {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|qid=Q6601|1=1558}}–{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|qid=Q6659|1=1570}}), lord of {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Castle|Q11647049|qid=Q11647049|1=Kanagawa Castle}} {{ill|lt=Matsuda Mototeru|Q11531997|qid=Q11531997|lt=Matsuda Sakon Shōgen Motokata|1=Matsuda Mototeru}} and his son {{ill|lt=Matsuda Motokata|Q11531996|qid=Q11531996|lt=Motokata|1=Matsuda Motokata}}, believers in {{ill|lt=Nichiren-shū|Q206611|qid=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}, pressed temples and shrines to convert and suppressed those who refused. Because only {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was treated as a deity invited by the Lotus teaching, this shrine is said to have escaped destruction by renaming itself Hirotaka Hachimangū. It was called by that name throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|qid=Q7741|1=1870}}), it resumed the name Miwa Shrine; in Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became gōsha. On January 27, Meiji 40 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|qid=Q2048|1=1907}}), it was designated a shrine receiving official offerings. On January 11, Meiji 43 ({{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|qid=Q2075|1=1910}}), it merged Aoki Shrine (village shrine; deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}’s rough spirit). ===Cultural properties=== ;Setouchi City designated important cultural properties :*Hirotaka Hachimangū inscribed roof tile :*:A tile excavated from Miwa Shrine precincts, with the inscription “{{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13, intercalary 7th month, 13th day...,” carved with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=spatula|1=UNKNOWN}}. :*Miwa Shrine yamamomo tree :*:An old {{ill|lt=yamamomo|Q311617|qid=Q311617|1=Myrica rubra}} tree about 300 years old, revered as a sacred tree. Base circumference about 7 m, height about 10 m. Designated by former Osafune Town on March 19, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|qid=Q1986|1=2003}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 15). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Oku District Board of Education, ''Oku-gun-shi Part 2'', edited by Kobayashi Kumao, 1915. *On-site explanatory board. ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Miwa-jinja (Higashisue, Setouchi)}} *[http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=12058 Miwa Shrine] - Okayama Prefectural Shrine Association {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miwa Shrine (Higashisue, Setouchi)]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Setouchi, Okayama]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bizen Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Setouchi, Okayama]]
